root.narration: Your team just scored a touchdown to make it 28-26 with the other team leading! After a touchdown, you normally kick the extra point (1 point, almost always good) to make it 28-27. But you could also go for a two-point conversion (2 points but much harder) to TIE the game 28-28. There's 1:45 left and you have no timeouts. What do you choose?

choices.text: Go for two! If you make it, the game is tied and you could win in overtime. If you only kick the extra point, you're still losing!
Kick the extra point to make it 28-27 and hope to get the ball back for a field goal.
Kick the extra point, then try an onside kick to get the ball back.

outcome.headline: Two-Point Conversion! Game Tied!
outcome.explanation: Going for two was the right math! Here's why: if you kick the extra point, you trail 28-27. With 1:45 left and no timeouts, getting the ball back AND driving into field goal range is really hard. But if you convert the two-point attempt, it's 28-28 and you go to overtime with a fresh start! Yes, two-point conversions are harder (about 50% success), but needing a field goal drive in under 2 minutes with no timeouts is even harder. Go for the tie!
outcome.whatToRemember: Go for 2 when: being down 1 (after the extra point) is almost as bad as being down 2. If getting the ball back for a FG is unlikely, tie it up!

keyTerms.definition: After a touchdown, running one play from the 2-yard line. Score = 2 extra points instead of kicking for 1.
A kick after a touchdown worth 1 point. Made about 94% of the time in the NFL.
Thinking about what the LIKELY outcome is, not just the best case. Helps you make smarter decisions!

outcome.headline: 28-27. Still Losing. Clock Running Out!
outcome.explanation: The extra point makes it 28-27, but you're STILL losing. Now you need to kick off, stop the other team (using up clock), get the ball back, drive into field goal range, and kick a field goal -- all in under 2 minutes with no timeouts. That's A LOT of things that need to go right. Going for 2 would have given you a simple 50/50 shot at tying the game right there, which is better odds than needing all those things to happen.
outcome.whatToRemember: Sometimes the 'safe' play (kicking the extra point) is actually riskier because it leaves you needing a miracle comeback!

keyTerms.definition: The percentage chance a team has of winning based on the current situation. Math helps you make the best choice!

outcome.headline: Onside Kick Failed! Game Over!
outcome.explanation: An onside kick only works about 10% of the time! So your plan was: kick the extra point, then recover an onside kick (10% chance), then drive for a field goal. That's a LOT of low-probability events chained together. Going for the two-point conversion gives you about a 50% chance of tying the game in ONE play. 50% on one play is way better than 10% on a kick plus more things needed after that!
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't chain together multiple low-probability plays when one higher-probability play (two-point conversion) could solve the problem!

keyTerms.definition: A short kickoff that the kicking team tries to recover. Very risky -- only works about 10% of the time!
